title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Show the code
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"

df_pd = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=10)
df = pl.from_pandas(df_pd)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Executive Summary
This report analyzes the Star Wars survey dataset and prepares it for a predictive machine learning model. The data was cleaned by removing unnecessary columns, handling missing values, and converting categorical variables into numeric format using one-hot encoding.
Two visualizations were recreated to validate the dataset: average movie rankings and the number of Star Wars fans versus non-fans. These visuals helped confirm that the dataset was loaded and prepared correctly.
A Random Forest model was used to predict whether a respondent has a household income of $50,000 or greater. The model achieved an accuracy of 58.33%, which is above the required 55% threshold, showing that survey responses can be used to make reasonable predictions about income.

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Methods
The Star Wars survey dataset was loaded from the course website and converted into a Polars dataframe. Because the file contained extra lines at the top, the first 10 rows were skipped when reading the data.
Next, I recreated two visuals from the article: average movie rankings and the number of Star Wars fans versus non-fans. These charts were used to confirm that the data loaded correctly.
For the machine learning portion, I removed unnecessary columns including RespondentID and email. Then I created a target variable to indicate whether a respondent had a household income of $50,000 or greater. Respondents in the $50,000 and higher categories were coded as 1, while all others were coded as 0.
After that, I removed the original household_income column and dropped rows with missing values. I then applied one-hot encoding so that the remaining categorical variables could be used in a Random Forest classification model.


Show the code
fan_counts = df.group_by("star_wars_fan").len()

ggplot(fan_counts, aes(
    x="star_wars_fan",
    y="len"
)) + \
    geom_bar(stat="identity") + \
    labs(
        title="Star Wars Fans vs Non-Fans",
        x="Fan Status",
        y="Count"
    )



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   





Show the code
rank_summary = pl.DataFrame({
    "movie": [
        "Episode I",
        "Episode II",
        "Episode III",
        "Episode IV",
        "Episode V",
        "Episode VI"
    ],
    "avg_rank": [
        df["rank_episode_I"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean(),
        df["rank_episode_II"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean(),
        df["rank_episode_III"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean(),
        df["rank_episode_IV"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean(),
        df["rank_episode_V"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean(),
        df["rank_episode_VI"].cast(pl.Float64, strict=False).mean()
    ]
})

ggplot(rank_summary, aes(x="movie", y="avg_rank")) + \
    geom_bar(stat="identity") + \
    coord_flip() + \
    labs(
        title="Average Ranking of Star Wars Movies",
        x="Movie",
        y="Average Rank"
    )



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   





Show the code
df_ml = df.drop([
    "RespondentID",
    "email"
])

df_ml = df_ml.with_columns(
    pl.when(
        pl.col("household_income").is_in([
            "$50,000 - $99,999",
            "$100,000 - $149,999",
            "$150,000+"
        ])
    )
    .then(1)
    .otherwise(0)
    .alias("target")
)

df_ml = df_ml.drop("household_income")

df_ml = df_ml.drop_nulls()

df_ml.head()

Show the code
df_pd = df_ml.to_pandas()

df_pd = pd.get_dummies(df_pd, drop_first=False)

X = df_pd.drop("target", axis=1)
y = df_pd["target"]

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Validation (Stretch)
To validate that the dataset matches the original FiveThirtyEight article, I recreated two key visualizations: average movie rankings and the number of Star Wars fans versus non-fans.
The first visualization shows the average ranking for each Star Wars episode. The results follow the same general pattern as the article, where the original trilogy (Episodes IV, V, and VI) tends to receive better rankings than the prequel trilogy. This confirms that the ranking data in the dataset aligns with the published results.
The second visualization shows the number of respondents who consider themselves Star Wars fans versus non-fans. The chart shows a clear majority of respondents identifying as fans, which is consistent with expectations from the article.
Although the visualizations are not exact replicas, they are close enough in structure and values to validate that the dataset is accurate and correctly loaded.


Show the code
fan_counts = df.group_by("star_wars_fan").len().sort("len", descending=True)

ggplot(fan_counts, aes(
    x="star_wars_fan",
    y="len"
)) + \
    geom_bar(stat="identity") + \
    labs(
        title="Star Wars Fans vs Non-Fans",
        x="Fan Status",
        y="Count"
    )



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   





Show the code
ggplot(rank_summary, aes(x="movie", y="avg_rank")) + \
    geom_bar(stat="identity") + \
    coord_flip() + \
    labs(
        title="Average Ranking of Star Wars Movies (Lower is Better)",
        x="Movie",
        y="Average Rank"
    )

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Results
A Random Forest Classifier was used to predict whether a respondent had a household income of $50,000 or greater. The dataset was split into training and testing sets, with 70% used for training and 30% used for testing.


Show the code
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42
)

model = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(X_test)

accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)




Show the code
accuracy


0.5833333333333334


The model achieved an accuracy of 58.33%, which is above the required 55% threshold. This shows that the model performs well enough to make reasonable predictions about income level based on survey responses.
Although the accuracy is modest, this is expected because preferences about Star Wars are not strongly related to income.

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Conclusion
This project demonstrated how survey data can be cleaned and prepared for machine learning. After removing unnecessary columns, creating a target variable, and encoding categorical variables, the model was able to predict whether a respondent had a household income of at least $50,000.
One limitation is that dropping missing values reduces the amount of available data. Additionally, preferences about Star Wars may not strongly relate to income, which limits prediction accuracy.
Overall, the model successfully met the required accuracy and showed that survey data can be used for basic predictive analysis.
